text.title: Viral Loop Types and Product Match Guide
text.summary: Seven viral loop types, their mechanics, canonical examples, and a product-type match guide for selecting the right primary loop.

list.item: Inherent: product requires or fails to deliver core value without inviting others (Slack, Zoom). Test: does the product fail to work without the invitee?
Word of mouth: users talk about the product unprompted (Apple, Notion). Requires a strong product story; cannot be engineered directly.
Incentivized: explicit rewards for referrals — cash, credits, features (Dropbox +500MB, Uber credits). Measurable, tunable, fraud-prone.
Social: user output is visible to non-users, creating FOMO (Spotify Wrapped, Wordle). Requires shareable artifacts.
Collaborative: product improves or requires collaboration, pulling in teammates (Figma, Notion docs). Natural for B2B / team tools.
Content: users create content that carries branding and CTAs (Canva designs, TikTok). Requires user-generated artifacts with embedded attribution.
Embedded: product name appears in output ("Made with X", "Sent via X"). Low friction, low K, good for awareness at scale.
B2C social / media: Social + Content loops
B2B team tools: Collaborative + Inherent loops
Productivity SaaS: Embedded + Word of mouth loops
Consumer subscription (low viral): Incentivized loop as primary

rule.statement: Pick ONE primary loop type and instrument it completely before designing a second; multi-loop products without a baseline K cannot diagnose regressions.
rule.rationale: Adding a second loop before the first is measured creates attribution confusion — you cannot tell which loop is driving new users or why K changed.

section.title: Loop Anatomy
section.p: Every loop has five structural elements. Define all five before engineering:

list.item: Action: what the user does that triggers the loop (completes a task, creates a document, achieves a milestone)
Artifact: what is created or shared as output (badge, report, invite link, branded content)
Distribution: how the artifact reaches potential new users (social share, email, in-product, search-indexable)
Motivation: why a new user would want to join after seeing the artifact (FOMO, utility, social connection)
Friction: steps to join from artifact exposure (target: one-click signup from invite link)
